title: Honoring the life and legacy of Henry Louis Aaron.
plain_summary: This bill is a way for Congress to honor and remember Hank Aaron, one of the greatest baseball players ever. In 1974, Aaron broke Babe Ruth's home run record, but he faced a lot of racism during his career. The House of Representatives wants to recognize Aaron not just as an amazing athlete, but also as someone who helped fight for civil rights and equality for Black Americans. The resolution celebrates everything Aaron did both in baseball and in working to make life better for others.
last_action_text: Referred to the House Committee on Oversight and Government Reform.

sections.title: Resolution Honoring the life and legacy of Henry Louis Aaron
sections.text:
Henry Louis Aaron, also called Hank Aaron, was born on February fifth, nineteen thirty-four in Mobile, Alabama. He was one of eight children born to Herbert Aaron Senior and Estella Aaron. Hank grew up during a time when laws kept Black people separated from white people, called Jim Crow laws. He faced racism and unfair treatment from a young age, including from fans in the stands when he played baseball.

Hank started his baseball career as a teenager playing in the Negro leagues, which were leagues for Black baseball players. He first played for the Prichard Athletics, then the Mobile Black Bears, and later for the Indianapolis Clowns.

In nineteen fifty-two, the Boston Braves signed Hank to play for their minor league team called the Eau Claire Bears. He made the All-Star team and was named Rookie of the Year by all the voters. In nineteen fifty-three, he was moved up to play for the Jacksonville Braves, where he was one of the first Black players in that league. He helped the team win the championship and won the Most Valuable Player Award.

In nineteen fifty-four, Hank was invited to spring training with the Milwaukee Braves and signed his first Major League Baseball contract. He made his first appearance in the major leagues against the Cincinnati Reds and hit his first major league home run on April twenty-third, nineteen fifty-four.

Hank won his first batting title in nineteen fifty-six and was voted the National League's Most Valuable Player in nineteen fifty-seven. He helped the Braves win their first pennant in Milwaukee and led them to victory in the nineteen fifty-seven World Series against the New York Yankees.

In nineteen sixty-six, Hank moved with the Braves to Atlanta, Georgia. He played twenty-three seasons in Major League Baseball, with all but his final two seasons being with the Braves in Milwaukee and then Atlanta.

On April fourth, nineteen seventy-four, Hank tied Babe Ruth's home run record by hitting his seven hundred fourteenth home run against the Cincinnati Reds on his first swing of the season. On the evening of April eighth, nineteen seventy-four, Hank made history by breaking Babe Ruth's record when he hit home run number seven hundred fifteen against the Los Angeles Dodgers at Atlanta-Fulton County Stadium. This made him the country's home run king.

During this historic moment, baseball announcer Vin Scully said it was a marvelous moment for baseball, Atlanta, Georgia, the country, and the world. He noted that a Black man was getting a standing ovation in the Deep South for breaking the record of a beloved baseball hero.

Hank went on to hit seven hundred fifty-five home runs in his career. This record was not broken for more than thirty years. His final home run came on July twentieth, nineteen seventy-six.

When Hank passed away, he held Major League Baseball records for the most career runs batted in with two thousand two hundred ninety-seven, extra base hits with one thousand four hundred seventy-seven, and total bases with six thousand eight hundred fifty-six. He was one of only four players to have at least seventeen seasons with one hundred fifty or more hits. He was second in home runs and at-bats with twelve thousand three hundred sixty-four. He was third in most games played with three thousand two hundred ninety-eight and hits with three thousand seven hundred seventy-one.

Hank achieved many other career highlights and awards. He became the ninth player to get three thousand hits and the first player to achieve both five hundred home runs and three thousand hits. He was a two-time National League batting champion and won the National League's single-season home run title four times. He had a career batting average of three hundred five thousandths. He was voted an All-Star in all but his first and last seasons and won three Gold Glove awards for his play as a right fielder.

Hank achieved these milestones while bravely facing racism at every stage of his career. He was not allowed to stay in hotels where his white teammates stayed when he played in the minor leagues. He received many racist letters and threats. He even needed protection from law enforcement at games to protect him from racist violence or harassment.

Hank accomplished his stellar career without using steroids or other drugs that help performance. He played baseball with the highest standard of honesty and fairness.

During his career, Hank became a national symbol for never giving up. He showed athletic greatness and strength while enduring terrible racism and hate. This helped advance the cause for civil rights and made him a civic leader in the Black community.

Hank became the first Black American to hold a senior management position in Major League Baseball as a front office worker with the Atlanta Braves. He supported the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People. He also started the Chasing the Dream Foundation to support poor youth through mentorship and money.

In April nineteen seventy-seven, the Atlanta Braves retired Hank's Number forty-four, built a statue in his honor in nineteen eighty-two, and named the address of their second home, Turner Field, as seven fifty-five Hank Aaron Drive.

Hank is an important part of Mobile, Alabama history. The city has honored him by building a stadium, opening a museum, and naming a park after him.

In nineteen eighty-two, his first year he could be chosen, Hank was elected to the Baseball Hall of Fame. He received ninety-seven and eight tenths percent of the vote, which was the second-highest number at the time, only behind Ty Cobb.

On the twenty-fifth anniversary of Hank's seven hundred fifteenth home run, Major League Baseball created the Hank Aaron Award. This award is given every year to the players with the best overall hitting performances in each league.

In two thousand two, Hank received the Presidential Medal of Freedom from President George W. Bush. This is the country's highest award for civilians. The citation noted that Hank embodied the true spirit of our country.

In two thousand fifteen, Hank was one of the first people to receive the Portrait of a Nation Prize from the National Portrait Gallery. This prize honors people who have achieved great things in civil rights, business, entertainment, science, and sports.

In two thousand twenty-two, the Library of Congress chose to preserve the television broadcast of Hank's seven hundred fifteenth home run in the National Recording Registry. This registry keeps events that are very important to the culture, history, or beauty of the United States.

When Hank passed away, he was known for having helped the Black community and improved relationships between people of different races throughout his career. He showed tremendous dignity and achieved great things even when facing racism and hate. This made his legacy as a leading figure for civil rights.

Therefore, the House of Representatives honors the life and legacy of Henry Louis Aaron for his accomplishments on and off the baseball field. It honors him for fighting against racism and breaking down racial barriers in the fight for equality as one of the last Major League Baseball All-Stars to have played in the Negro leagues.

The House of Representatives states that Henry Louis Aaron truly embodied the spirit and promise of the country. He reflected the best of the determination and never-give-up attitude of the people of the United States. He showed the unbreakable will of Black Americans to overcome impossible challenges to achieve greatness even when facing constant unfairness and racism.

The House of Representatives recognizes the life and legacy of Henry Louis Aaron as an important figure in the fight for civil rights. It also recognizes him as one of the greatest and most successful baseball players and professional athletes of the United States of all time.
